original_hypothesis.title: Expired Lock Merge into SMNFT Grants Free Bonus Voting Power
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: The merge function does not check that the source token has an active lock for non-permanent sources. An expired non-permanent lock can be merged into an SMNFT target, burning the expired tokens and granting SMNFT bonus voting power (10% by default). The user effectively converts withdrawable tokens into permanent voting power with a free bonus, when the intent is that SMNFT conversions require commitment (non-expired tokens).
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: The merge function only checks `_locked1.end > block.timestamp || _locked1.isPermanent` for the destination, not the source. Users can merge expired locks to gain bonus voting power without the intended time-lock commitment.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Only _locked1 (destination) checked for expiration: require(_locked1.end > block.timestamp || _locked1.isPermanent). No corresponding check for _locked0 (source) when !_locked0.isPermanent.

preconditions: User owns an expired normal veNFT (source) and an SMNFT (destination)
Source lock not voted or attached

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: User can withdraw expired 1000 tokens OR merge into SMNFT for 1000 + 100 bonus = 1100 voting power permanently
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Let locks expire intentionally, then merge into SMNFT for 10% free bonus
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Free 10% voting power bonus on all expired tokens merged

economic_questions: What is the economic value of 10% permanent voting power bonus vs token value?
How many users could exploit this to accumulate disproportionate voting power?
Does this distort gauge voting and bribe incentives?
validation_requirements: Confirm merge succeeds with expired source lock
Check if the burned tokens are properly accounted

preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: 10% bonus voting power on all expired tokens
preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_profit_margin: 10% permanent voting power gain per conversion

market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Gradual inflation of SMNFT voting power

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: All other veNFT holders via diluted voting power
potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: Proportional dilution based on attacker's merged amount

investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Test merge with expired source into SMNFT destination

validation_result.impact_summary: Merging an expired lock into an SMNFT burns the tokens in exchange for permanent voting power, which is economically identical to withdrawing the expired lock and depositing the tokens into the SMNFT.
validation_result.detailed_analysis:
The hypothesis claims that merging an expired lock into an SMNFT grants a 'free bonus' without the intended commitment. However, this is based on a misunderstanding of the SMNFT mechanics. When an expired lock is merged into an SMNFT, the `merge` function explicitly burns the tokens (`assert(_black.burn(value0))`). This is the ultimate commitment, as the user permanently loses access to the principal tokens in exchange for permanent voting power and the SMNFT bonus.

This behavior is economically identical to the user withdrawing their expired lock (receiving liquid tokens) and then calling `deposit_for` on their SMNFT. In `deposit_for`, liquid tokens deposited into an SMNFT are also burned (`assert(_black.burnFrom(from,_value))`) and the same 10% bonus is applied. Therefore, the lack of an expiration check on the source lock in `merge` is not a defect; it simply allows users to convert their expired locks into SMNFTs in a single transaction rather than requiring a separate `withdraw` and `deposit_for`.
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: The `merge` function correctly handles the conversion by burning the source tokens when the destination is an SMNFT. The `deposit_for` function confirms that liquid tokens can be deposited into an SMNFT at any time to receive the bonus, proving that there is no requirement for tokens to be in an active time-lock prior to SMNFT conversion.

vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: This code shows that when a non-SMNFT (including an expired lock) is merged into an SMNFT, the tokens are permanently burned via `_black.burn(value0)`. The user pays the full price for the SMNFT bonus by destroying their tokens, which is the intended design.
This confirms that any user with liquid tokens can deposit them into an SMNFT, burning them in the process to receive the bonus. An expired lock is equivalent to liquid tokens.

execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: When `merge` is called with an expired `_from` lock and an SMNFT `_to` lock, the function bypasses the expiration check because `_locked1.isPermanent` is true. It then calculates the bonus, adds it to the destination lock, and calls `_black.burn(value0)` to destroy the tokens from the expired lock. This is the correct and intended behavior for SMNFT conversions.

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: The proposed attack scenario involves letting a lock expire and then merging it into an SMNFT to gain a 'free' 10% bonus. This is invalid because the tokens are burned during the merge. The user sacrifices 100% of their principal to gain a 110% permanent voting power position, which is the exact intended economic exchange for SMNFTs.
validation_result.impact_assessment: None. The system functions as designed and there is no economic exploit or bypass of intended commitments.
